food_subgroup: Tortilla chip-based snack bowls with cheese sauce and toppings

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 covers nacho food products as a category. No exact term exists for a loaded cheesy nacho bowl with multiple toppings; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Corn chips and similar snack products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKW covers corn chips and similar snack products. No specific code exists for assembled nacho bowls with cheese sauce and toppings; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for loaded cheesy nacho bowls as a composite dish.

preparation_method: baked or fried tortilla chips as base

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, representing a full nacho bowl as typically served at a fast-casual restaurant such as Subway Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Nachos with cheese sauce — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Loaded Cheesy Nacho Bowls' exists in any approved primary source. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for nachos with cheese sauce (FDC ID 1100613, 'Nachos, with cheese') and adjusted for a loaded bowl with additional toppings (beans, sour cream, salsa, jalapeños). The composite estimate reflects a weighted average of the base nacho-with-cheese profile and typical loaded topping contributions. This is an estimated match; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Corn chips, plain — Glycemic Index Database; and tortilla/corn chip GI analogous estimate for nacho bowl
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a loaded cheesy nacho bowl. The GI of 55 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for corn chips / tortilla chips (GI approximately 42–63, mean ~55). According to the University of Sydney GI Database classification, a GI of 55 is considered low. The loaded bowl adds cheese sauce, beans, sour cream, and salsa, which collectively lower the effective GI relative to plain chips due to fat, protein, and fibre contributions; a GI of 55 is therefore a conservative central estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (55 × 85.75) / 100 ≈ 47.1 g, where available carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 24.5 × 3.5 = 85.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (420 mg per 100 g; approximately 1470 mg per 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid loaded nacho bowls due to the very high sodium load from processed cheese sauce, salsa, and seasoned chips, which can significantly elevate blood pressure; a single serving may provide more than 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (GL ≈ 47) and refined carbohydrate base from tortilla chips
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with loaded nacho bowls because the large serving size delivers a high glycemic load of approximately 47, driven by the refined corn chip base, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; portion control and pairing with additional fibre or protein sources is strongly advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (4.2 g per 100 g), trans fat (0.2 g per 100 g), and high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of loaded cheesy nacho bowls because the combination of saturated fat from processed cheese, potential trans fat from fried chips, and high sodium content collectively raises LDL cholesterol and blood pressure, increasing cardiovascular risk; occasional consumption in small portions is preferable to regular intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Loaded Cheesy Nacho Bowls are a Western fast-casual snack dish originating from American Tex-Mex cuisine, consisting of a base of crispy tortilla chips (made from corn masa) topped generously with warm processed cheese sauce, and loaded with additional toppings such as jalapeños, sour cream, salsa, black beans, and optionally seasoned ground beef or grilled chicken. The dish is served in a bowl format, making it a self-contained meal or substantial snack. The texture profile combines the crunch of the chips with the creamy richness of the cheese sauce and the cooling contrast of sour cream. The flavour is savoury, salty, mildly spicy from jalapeños, and tangy from salsa. In Singapore, this dish is available at fast-casual outlets such as Subway and mix-and-match food stalls, typically priced around SGD 7.20. Nutritionally, a 350 g serving provides approximately 770 kcal, 22.75 g protein, 40.25 g fat, and 85.75 g carbohydrate, with a high glycemic load of approximately 47. The dish is high in sodium (approximately 1470 mg per serving) and saturated fat, making it a high-concern food for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. It is not suitable for vegans or those avoiding dairy or gluten. The dish is ultra-processed due to the use of processed cheese sauce and commercially produced tortilla chips.
common_user_queries: What are loaded cheesy nacho bowls?
How many calories in a nacho bowl?
Is nacho bowl high in fat?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for 'Loaded Cheesy Nacho Bowls' was found in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, USDA FoodData Central exact match). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for nachos with cheese (FDC ID 1100613) adjusted for a loaded bowl with multiple toppings. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using corn chip / tortilla chip analogues. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central 'Nachos, with cheese' (FDC ID 1100613) as the closest approved source match, adjusted upward for additional toppings (beans, sour cream, salsa, jalapeños) typical of a loaded nacho bowl. This is an estimated composite; values should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis or a branded product specification if available. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 (sentinel value) because it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entry for nachos with cheese. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from the USDA FoodData Central composite and are approximate. 4. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using corn chip / tortilla chip data; no direct GI measurement for a loaded cheesy nacho bowl was found in any approved source. 5. The food_group has been corrected from the input value 'Vegetables' to 'Snacks', which is the appropriate classification for a tortilla chip-based snack bowl. 6. The cuisine has been corrected from the input value 'Sandwich' to 'Western', reflecting the Tex-Mex / American fast-casual origin of this dish. 7. Multilingual names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are provided in accordance with the Singapore multilingual naming rule, as this dish is served in Singapore. 8. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule does not apply.
